EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving into Southern New England was accompanied by an unstable airmass with relatively cold temperatures aloft for June. This allowed for several hail-producing thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level low pressure system located just to the North of New England aided in the development of strong to locally severe thunderstorms on the 11th. The main result was wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees downed in Manchester and Broad Brook. One tree crushed a car in Broad Brook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary coupled with an upper level disturbance allowed thunderstorms, some severe, to develop the night of August 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down across power lines on route 75.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary coupled with an upper level disturbance allowed thunderstorms, some severe, to develop the night of August 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages due to downed trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary coupled with an upper level disturbance allowed thunderstorms, some severe, to develop the night of August 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches down on South Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong instability, moderate to strong wind shear, and the approach of a cold front set the stage for a widespread thunderstorm outbreak across Massachusetts, Connecticut, and Rhode Island on the 16th. The main result was wind damage to trees and power lines. This was the most widespread severe thunderstorm event of May 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees downed in Coventry, Eagleville, and Mansfield. The damage|in Coventry was found to have occurred from a microburst with estimated 60 to 80 mph winds. The most pronounced damage was found to be near the|shores of Eagleville Lake where at least 100 trees were downed - some crushing cars and parts of homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front was the trigger for thunderstorms on the 2nd of June. The airmass was unstable out ahead of the front. The atmopheric column contained a moderate amount of wind shear which acted to organize the thunderstorms and enhance their severity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail reported in Windsor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped hail varying in size from pennies to golf balls in Southington. A few cars sustained hail damage from some of the bigger hailstones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong instability, moderate to strong wind shear, and the approach of a cold front set the stage for a widespread thunderstorm outbreak across Massachusetts, Connecticut, and Rhode Island on the 16th. The main result was wind damage to trees and power lines. This was the most widespread severe thunderstorm event of May 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches downed in West Hartford and East Windsor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, humid airmass, combined with the approach of a cold front from the northwest, led to the development of isolated thunderstorms across portions of western New England during the afternoon and early evening hours of Friday June 1st. A few of these storms became locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Watertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the southern Plains, into the central Great Lakes on Sunday, December 23rd, while intensifying. The low pressure system then moved into eastern Canada by Monday, December 24th. A cold front associated with this low pressure system swept east across eastern New York and western New England late Sunday afternoon and evening.||Strong to locally damaging winds occurred ahead of, and with the cold frontal passage on Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in Litchfield due to high winds. In addition, numerous power outages were reported across the county as a result of downed tree limbs and wires due to high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Tuesday February 13th, and then tracked northeast, reaching the mid Atlantic coast by Wednesday morning on February 14th, while intensifying. The storm continued to strengthen during the day on February 14th, before moving off the New England coast by Wednesday night. |This storm led to a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across northwest Connecticut. Snow and sleet accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were reported, mainly across the higher elevations of northern Litchfield County.|The precipitation began during the late evening hours of Tuesday, and ended Wednesday evening.

A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Tuesday February 13th, and then tracked northeast, reaching the mid Atlantic coast by Wednesday morning on February 14th, while intensifying. The storm continued to strengthen during the day on February 14th, before moving off the New England coast by Wednesday night. |This storm led to a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain across northwest Connecticut. Snow and sleet accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were reported, mainly across the higher elevations of northern Litchfield County.|The precipitation began during the late evening hours of Tuesday, and ended Wednesday evening.

An unusually strong and slow moving coastal storm for mid April tracked to western Long Island Sound on April 16th before weakening slowly and drifting offshore. This storm brought strong winds and widespread river and stream flooding to northern Connecticut.|Northeast winds gusted as high as 55 mph in the higher elevations of Tolland and Windham Counties, which brought down branches in North Grosvenor Dale and a tree in Coventry.|Rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches, combined with wet antecedent conditions, resulted in widespread river and stream flooding, as well as significant flooding of urban areas. Minor to moderate flooding occurred on the Farmington and mainstem Connecticut Rivers. The highest rainfall total reported was 5.75 inches in Burlington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded in Granby. The Mockanum River in Manchester came out of its banks and threatened nearby homes. In Bristol, flooding was reported along a portion of Route 72. Several businesses were also flooded and Memorial Boulevard was closed. |Minor to moderate flooding also affected the larger mainstem rivers. The Connecticut River at Thompsonville crested at 7 feet at 3 pm on the 17th (flood stage is 5 feet), and at Hartford it crested at 23.4 feet at 1215 pm on the 18th (flood stage is 16 feet). On the Farmington River, a crest of 16.6 feet was recorded at Simsbury at 915 am on the 17th (flood stage is 12 feet), while at Tarriffville the river crested at 9.9 feet at 430 pm on the 17th (flood stage is 9 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong and slow moving coastal storm for mid April tracked to western Long Island Sound on April 16th before weakening slowly and drifting offshore. This storm brought strong winds and widespread river and stream flooding to northern Connecticut.|Northeast winds gusted as high as 55 mph in the higher elevations of Tolland and Windham Counties, which brought down branches in North Grosvenor Dale and a tree in Coventry.|Rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches, combined with wet antecedent conditions, resulted in widespread river and stream flooding, as well as significant flooding of urban areas. Minor to moderate flooding occurred on the Farmington and mainstem Connecticut Rivers. The highest rainfall total reported was 5.75 inches in Burlington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were downed in Coventry, one near Harkney Mill Road and the other near Pigmin Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the southeast states during Thursday afternoon on March 15th, and then tracked northeast while intensifying, reaching the Delmarva coast by Friday evening on March 16th, and off the New England coast by Saturday morning on March 17th. |Snow began during the mid to late morning hours of Friday, before tapering off early Saturday morning. Sleet and freezing drizzle mixed in with the snow Friday night and early Saturday morning, especially across southern Litchfield county. Total snowfall accumulations across Litchfield county ranged from 8 to 12 inches, with 11 inches reported at Norfolk, 8.5 inches at Torrington, and 7.5 inches at Thomaston.|This storm had significant adverse impacts to travel and commerce across the region from Friday afternoon into early Saturday morning. Travel was treacherous for the Friday evening commute, with numerous accidents reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on power lines north of Sherman near Route 55.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced torrential rain that resulted in significant street flooding in Brookfield of Northern Fairfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain caused flash flooding along portions of Candlewood Lake Road, Main Drive, Pocono Road, and Dean Road in the town of Brookfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front traversed eastern New York and western New England during Thursday May 31st. This front then gradually lifted to the northeast as a warm front by late Thursday afternoon and evening.||This front, combined with several weak upper level atmospheric disturbances passing through, produced numerous showers and thunderstorms across the region, some of which became locally severe Thursday afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported during a thunderstorm in Harwinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing wave of low pressure traveled east along a nearly stationary front during the late morning and afternoon hours of Wednesday May 16th. This triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York State into western New England. Some thunderstorms became locally severe. The low pressure system, and attendant cold front and thunderstorms, moved southeast of the region by Wednesday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees near the ski area in Woodbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late season Nor'easter impacted the region with a period of moderate coastal flooding.  The low orignated over the southern plains on Friday, April 13, and then reached the mid Atlantic coast Sunday morning, April 15.  From there, it underwent rapid deepening and tracked northeast, reaching the waters off southern New England Sunday night.  The combination of a strong high off the New England coast and a period of higher than normal spring tides resulted in tidal piling across Long Island Sound.||Tidal departures were highest on western Long Island Sound from Sunday evening through Monday morning, ranging from 2.5 to 3.5 feet. ||At Stamford, Connecticut, there was moderate coastal flooding with the highest tide level 11.34 feet (MLLW) at 10:30 p.m. on Sunday, April 15. This was comparable to tide levels reached during the March 1993 Nor'easter and Hurricane Donna.||Several days prior to this event, model storm surge forecasts were as high as 4.5 feet. For Long Island Sound, such a surge would have produced flooding approaching the level of the December 1992 Nor'easter. While these levels were not realized due to changes in the forecast track, this storm resulted in considerable damage to property.

A low pressure system developed along a front over the eastern Great Lakes Thursday morning, and then headed northeast while intensifying. As the low strengthened, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed within the warm sector of this system over northwest Connecticut during the afternoon hours of Thursday July 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a funnel cloud extending approximately three quarters of the way from the cloud base to the ground in New Preston during a thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service meteorologists conducted a storm survey in conjunction with 5 emergency coordinators, the highway superintendent and emergency management officials from New Milford to investigate damage in the Gaylordsville and New Milford areas.||They determined that damaging straight line winds within a thunderstorm, with estimated winds of 85 to 95 mph, downed numerous pine and poplar trees on Straight Rock Drive and Long Mountain Road. The trees were knocked down and uprooted all in one direction, indicating that straight line winds caused the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the Great Lakes into southeast Canada on Tuesday morning. A warm front associated with this low moved across eastern New York and western New England early Tuesday morning. With cold air initially in place, a mix of light snow, sleet and freezing rain developed before daybreak Tuesday, before changing to rain by midday Tuesday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain developed during the early morning hours of Tuesday November 20th, before changing to rain by midday Tuesday. Snow and sleet accumulations of up to an inch fell, which, combined with a coating of ice from freezing rain, led to hazardous driving conditions during the Tuesday morning commute.

This low then tracked into the Canadian maritimes by Monday morning.||Total snow and sleet amounts of 1 to 3 inches fell during this storm across Litchfield County, in addition to a few tenths of an inch of ice accretion from freezing rain.||The wintry mix created treacherous travel conditions Sunday into Monday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure across the St. Lawrence Valley locked in cold air across southern New England and combined with low pressure south of the coast to produce a significant snowfall.  Snow fell at rates of up to two inches per hour for an eight to ten hour period resulting in nearly a foot of snow in most locations.  Many motorists were affected as early dismissals from work and school just before snow began created rush hour like conditions which limited the snowplows' ability to plow.

High pressure across the St. Lawrence Valley locked in cold air across southern New England and combined with low pressure south of the coast to produce a significant snowfall.  Snow fell at rates of up to two inches per hour for an eight to ten hour period resulting in nearly a foot of snow in most locations.  Many motorists were affected as early dismissals from work and school just before snow began created rush hour like conditions which limited the snowplows' ability to plow.

A low pressure system developed along a nearly stationary boundary over the Ohio Valley on Sunday, December 9th, and moved east northeast to a position just south of Long Island by Monday morning on December 10th.||This system spread a swath of snow, sleet and freezing rain across the region from late Sunday afternoon into Monday morning.||Snow and sleet accumulations generally ranged from an inch or less. However, ice accretion of one tenth of an inch, to one third of an inch occurred. This wintry mix led to hazardous driving conditions for the Monday morning commute, and also led to numerous delayed openings, or closings of schools and businesses on Monday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system traveled east northeast, from the Ohio Valley on Sunday, to a position just south of Long Island by Monday morning.|This spread a swath of freezing rain and sleet across the Litchfield Hills from Sunday evening, into midday Monday.||Snow and sleet accumulations generally ranged from an inch or less. However, ice accretion of one tenth of an inch, to one third of an inch occurred. This wintry mix led to hazardous driving conditions for the Monday morning commute, and also led to numerous delayed openings, or closings of schools and businesses on Monday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east to northeast winds blowing across a long distance of Long Island Sound combined with high astronomical tides caused moderate coastal flooding across parts of Connecticut's Western Long Island Sound shores.  Tidal departures ranged from 3 to 5 feet above normal during high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate coastal flooding occurred in Guilford, along the shores of Long Island Sound.  Photographs showed flooding on Neck Road, opposite Guilford Harbor and approaching the State Boat Ramp on the East River at 3:30 pm.  Flooding occurred at 4 locations along Route 146 from the West River in Guilford to Stony Creek in Branford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Lower Mississippi Valley Wednesday evening, December 12, 2007, then tracked northeast toward the Middle Atlantic States. As the upper trough approached the Eastern Seaboard on Thursday morning, December 13, a secondary low developed along the Middle Atlantic coast. This low tracked to the south and east of Long Island during the evening hours. Polar high pressure preceding the storm dropped southeast across eastern Canada and into the Northeast Wednesday night, providing enough cold air for a predominatly snow event for the interior and a wintry mix at the coast. Northern portions of New Haven County in southwest Connecticut received 5 to 9 inches, with Wolcott receiving 9 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretion downed tree limbs and wires, causing power outages across much of Hartford County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a ridge of high pressure extending from north of the Great Lakes southeast across southern New England moved slowly northeast, a low pressure system developed off the Carolina coast.  As this low moved north to northeast just off the mid Atlantic coast, it rapidly intensified during Wednesday morning, February 14.  This low moved across eastern Long Island during the early afternoon hours. |These systems produced snow, sleet, freezing rain, rain, strong gusty winds, and minor tidal flooding across the region. |Light snow developed from southwest to northeast early Wednesday morning.  By 6 a.m., a mixed bag of snow, sleet, and freezing rain occurred everywhere with temperatures in the lower 20s across southern Connecticut. |As the deepening low moved more to the northeast, cold air was locked in west of the storm track, prompting the issuance of Ice Storm Warnings for Southern Farfield, Southern New Haven, Middlesex, and Northern New London Counties between 10 a.m. and 10:15 a.m.  Six hour precipitation totals between 7 a.m. and 1 p.m. were between 0.50 and 0.75 inches. This fell as mainly plain rain across coastal New London and mainly as a combo of snow, sleet and freezing rain elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began early Wednesday morning on Feb. 14, mixing with freezing rain at times. A mixture of heavy snow, sleet, and freezing rain developed by mid morning and continued through mid afternoon before ending as some light snow. The accumulation of 2 to 3 inches of snow and ice caused major mass transit problems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure strengthened rapidly as it tracked from the mid Atlantic coast to Cape Cod. Snow began in northern Connecticut late in the evening on the 13th, before changing to a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain during the morning of the 14th. Although snowfall totals only averaged 2 to 5 inches, this was the season's first winter storm, and the combination of snow and ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions, especially for morning commuters.|Officially, the snowfall total at Bradley International Airport in Windsor Locks was 2.4 inches. Other snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters and cooperative observers, included 5.2 inches in Tolland, 4.5 inches in North Granby and Vernon, 3.5 inches in Manchester, Staffordville, and Thompson, and 3 inches in Avon, Burlington, East Granby, and Somers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 3 inches in Somers to 5.2 inches in Tolland. Light icing was also reported from a combination of snow, sleet, and freezing rain which ended as a period of freezing drizzle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the mid Mississippi Valley on Thursday morning, March 1st, and then intensified, while reaching the western Great Lakes by Thursday evening. A warm front attached to this low approached eastern New York and western New England from the south late Thursday night, accompanied by a mixture of snow and sleet. Another low pressure system developed along the approaching warm front over eastern Maryland by Friday morning, March 2nd, which then strengthened and tracked northeast, reaching the northern New England coast by Friday evening. |As warm air aloft moved into the region, while cold and dry air lingered near the surface, a significant mixture of sleet and freezing rain occurred over northern Litchfield county. Ice accretions slightly in excess of one half of an inch led to downed tree limbs and powerlines across portions of northern Litchfield county, as well as subsequent power outages.|This storm had an adverse impact on travel across the region during Friday, particularly during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain led to ice accretions of up to one half inch across the higher elevations of northern Litchfield county.  The freezing rain, initially mixed with some sleet, began early Friday morning, and then changed to plain rain by midday.|There were several reports of downed tree limbs and power lines as a result of the ice accretion, particularly in Norfolk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the region during the morning of March 2, followed by a cold front in the afternoon.  This produced widespread heavy rain across Southern CT, with storm totals ranging from 1.08 to 3.91 inches.  Widespread flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads closed in Wallingford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slipped south of the tri-state area during the day on Thursday, March 15.  This allowed colder air to work into the region after several days of anomalously warm temperatures.  Farther to the south, along the Gulf Coast, low pressure developed and deepened as it tracked northeast along the boundary.  The deepening low tracked to near the Mid Atlantic coast Friday evening, March 16, to just east of Montauk Point Saturday morning, March 17. |Initially, the precipitation started off as a light mix of snow, sleet, and rain during the early morning hours, but, as the colder air continued to deepen, the preciptation fell primarily as snow during the daytime hours, falling moderate to occasionally heavy at times.  During the evening hours, the precipitation changed over to sleet and eventually to freezing rain. |Snow and sleet totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches, with the highest amounts across interior Fairfield and New Haven counties.  The NWS Cooperative Observer in Bridgeport measured 6.0 inches.|In addition, there were major impacts on mass transit with high winds and heavy wintry precipitation during the evening rush-hour traffic. Hundreds of flights were cancelled at the metropolitan airports with many planes stuck on the tarmac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Carolinas strengthened as it tracked over southeast Massachusetts and Cape Cod Bay. This winter storm brought heavy snow and sleet to northern Connecticut, with totals of 6 to 12 inches on average, before changing to sleet and freezing rain as the storm wound down. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 8 inches in West Hartford to 12 inches in North Granby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing wave of low pressure traveled east along a nearly stationary front during the late morning and afternoon hours of Wednesday May 16th. This triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York State into western New England. Some thunderstorms became locally severe. The low pressure system, and attendant cold front and thunderstorms, moved southeast of the region by Wednesday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in Burrville and other sections of Torrington. In Torrington, the trees were reported down on Guerdat Road, Newfield Road, and on Wilson Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado occurred in Somers on May 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Landspout, technically a tornado because it was a rotating column of air in contact with the ground and a cumuliform cloud, caused damage to a commercial barn at the intersection of Hall Hill Road and Brace Road. No injuries were reported. The rating is EF0 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale...with winds around 70 mph. 4 people witnessed a debris funnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong instability, moderate to strong wind shear, and the approach of a cold front set the stage for a widespread thunderstorm outbreak across Massachusetts, Connecticut, and Rhode Island on the 16th. The main result was wind damage to trees and power lines. This was the most widespread severe thunderstorm event of May 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bow echo downed numerous trees across Windham County. The most pronounced damage occurred in Pomfret where a microburst occurred. Hundreds of trees were downed in the vicinity of Taft Pond Road and Route 97. Some of the trees were sheared off well above ground level. Winds were estimated in this area to be between 60 and 80 mph. Tree Damage also occurred in Ashford, Putnam, and Killingly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front traversed eastern New York and western New England during Thursday May 31st. This front then gradually lifted to the northeast as a warm front by late Thursday afternoon and evening.||This front, combined with several weak upper level atmospheric disturbances passing through, produced numerous showers and thunderstorms across the region, some of which became locally severe Thursday afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down in Winsted due to thunderstorm winds. Two trees reportedly fell on homes, and another two trees fell on a sports utility vehicle on Route 44 outside of a restaurant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front the afternoon of the 16th...with 1 particularly potent bow echo that moved across northern Fairfield, New Haven, and Middlesex Counties. This bow echo produced a microburst and an EF1 tornado in Fairfield County, and another microburst in Middlesex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down with an estimated 60-70 mph wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The microburst damage had a nearly 2 mile path, between Redding and Ridgfield, that began about 100 yards in width and fanned out to nearly 300 yards as it extended from a southwest to northeast direction. Based on the damage observed, the estimated wind speeds were 60 to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, humid airmass, combined with the approach of a cold front from the northwest, led to the development of isolated thunderstorms across portions of western New England during the afternoon and early evening hours of Friday June 1st. A few of these storms became locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 year old man working inside his garage was leaning against a metal sink when lightning struck outside. The lightning traveled through metal pipes, hit the sink, then hit his belt buckle and exited out his foot.  He sustained a small exit wound on his big toe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor���Easter occurred during Sunday and Monday, April 15th and 16th.  It brought heavy rain and high winds that caused widespread and significant river, stream, and urban flooding of low lying and poor drainage areas.  High winds downed many trees and power lines across Southeast Connecticut. Low pressure developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Saturday April 14th, and then moved northeast while intensifying, reaching the southern Appalachians by Sunday morning, April 15th, and then just south of western Long Island by Monday morning, April 16th. This low became very intense, with a central barometric pressure falling below 970 millibars upon reaching just south of Long Island Monday morning. The low then headed off the New England coast by Tuesday morning. |This intense coastal storm spread heavy precipitation across northwest Connecticut, starting on Sunday, and persisting into late Monday. Initially, the precipitation fell as a mixture of wet snow, sleet and rain, with snow and sleet more prevalent across the higher elevations. The precipitation then changed to plain rain by late Sunday afternoon into Monday. Liquid equivalent precipitation total from this storm ranged from 3 to 7 inches. This led to widespread flooding across northwest Connecticut from late Sunday into Monday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to widespread flooding across Litchfield County from Sunday evening on the 15th, and persisted through Wednesday morning on the 18th.  Several streams and creeks exceeded bankful as a result of this heavy rain, including the Still River in Winsted, and the Nepaug Brook and River in New Hartford.  This led to numerous road closures across the county, as well as some evacuations.|Some of the roads that were closed included Carpenter Road in new Hartford, where a foot of water was reported covering the bridge just north of Route 202, South Main Street and Highland Lake Road in Torrington, where some debris also covered portions of Highland Lake Road, Route 47 and Weekeepeemee Road in Woodbury, Torrington Road between Torrington and Winsted, Cross Road and Youngsfield Road in New Milford, as well as several roads in Washington and Winsted that were washed out.|In addition, several evacuations also occurred, including residents on Standard Avenue in Winsted, as well as at a 40 unit apartment complex in Winsted.|A mudslide also was reported at Grove Street in New Milford by an Emergency Manager. This resulted in the evacuation of 5 homes.|The runoff from this heavy rainfall also led to moderate flooding on the Housatonic River.  At Falls Village, the Housatonic River crested at 11.14 feet at 0115 EST on the 18th, while at Gaylordsville, the river crested at 12.97 feet at 2300 EST on the 16th, Further south, the Housatonic River at Stevenson Dam crested at 19.96 feet at 1400 EST on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced high winds and torrential rain that resuled in wind damage in parts of New Haven and Middlesex Counties and flash flooding in and around North Haven.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced torrential rain that closed Old Firehouse Road in Naugatuck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced high winds and torrential rain that resuled in wind damage in parts of New Haven and Middlesex Counties and flash flooding in and around North Haven.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced high winds that downed trees along I-91 southbound after Exit 9, which resulted in closure of the right lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced high winds and torrential rain that resuled in wind damage in parts of New Haven and Middlesex Counties and flash flooding in and around North Haven.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced high winds that downed a large tree in Haddam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced high winds and torrential rain that resuled in wind damage in parts of New Haven and Middlesex Counties and flash flooding in and around North Haven.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced high winds that downed trees on Hartford Turnpike and Skiff Street.  A tree was downed on Mowry Street.  A tree fell on wires at Orchard Court and wires were downed on Bedford Avenue.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level low pressure system located just to the North of New England aided in the development of strong to locally severe thunderstorms on the 11th. The main result was wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches downed in Tolland and Somers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level disturbance moved east, across eastern New York State and western New England during the early to mid afternoon hours on Friday July 6th. This strong disturbance, combined with modest low and mid level instability, led to the development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across the region, beginning around midday, and persisting through the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Torrington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system slowly moved north from the Mid-Atlantic coast across New England and produced flash flooding across portions of Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of many streets and basements occurred in Stamford, Norwalk, and New Canaan.  Damage occurred to several hundred homes in the Darien and Stamford areas.||Storm Total Rainfall amounts of 5.40 inches was measured at New Canaan and 5.10 inches at Stamford by the Automated Weather Station.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved east toward the region, it produced heavy rain that caused flash flooding across portions of Southern Fairfield County in Connecticut.||A narrow band of heavy showers and thunderstorms was oriented from the south southwest to the north northeast across Southern Fairfield County.  The highest radar estimated rainfall rates were 1.5 to 2.0 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused the closure of portions of Route 123 in Norwalk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure, coupled with an upper level disturbance caused scattered severe storms across southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and trees down on Colchester Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure, coupled with an upper level disturbance caused scattered severe storms across southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Middletown Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure moving along a stalled frontal boundary produced flooding rains and scattered severe weather across southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 8 closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cool temperatures aloft spread over Southern New England during the afternoon hours of the 6th. These cool temperatures combined with moderate surface heating to allow for a good amount of instability to develop. Thunderstorms developed in this airmass ahead of a weak cold front and one became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree and wires down on Griswold Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, combined with the passage of a potent upper atmospheric disturbance, led to the development of scattered showers and embedded thunderstorms during Friday evening on August 17th. Strong winds accompanied these showers and thunderstorms, with some gusts of 50 mph and higher observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down due to strong thunderstorm winds in Canaan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, combined with the passage of a potent upper atmospheric disturbance, led to the development of scattered showers and embedded thunderstorms during Friday evening on August 17th. Strong winds accompanied these showers and thunderstorms, with some gusts of 50 mph and higher observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported to have fallen on a house on Route 44 in Norfolk due to strong thunderstorm winds. In addition, wires were also reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, combined with the passage of a potent upper atmospheric disturbance, led to the development of scattered showers and embedded thunderstorms during Friday evening on August 17th. Strong winds accompanied these showers and thunderstorms, with some gusts of 50 mph and higher observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down due to strong thunderstorm winds in Woodbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, combined with the passage of a potent upper atmospheric disturbance, led to the development of scattered showers and embedded thunderstorms during Friday evening on August 17th. Strong winds accompanied these showers and thunderstorms, with some gusts of 50 mph and higher observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Salisbury due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front the afternoon of the 16th...with 1 particularly potent bow echo that moved across northern Fairfield, New Haven, and Middlesex Counties. This bow echo produced a microburst and an EF1 tornado in Fairfield County, and another microburst in Middlesex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down near the Rock Ridge Country Club, just north of Route 302.  It traveled east to northeast and passed just south of the main town center in Newtown.  The tornado passed across Route 25, South Main Street and lifted along Sugarloaf Road, between Toddy Hill Road and Berkshire Drive.||High winds damaged many trees which fell through houses and cars along this path, which was approximately 4.5 miles with an average path width of 100 yards.  There was a well defined narrow track of discontinuous damage, where the damage converged in toward the track center.  Eye witness reports of funnel clouds were observed.||

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the Berkshires around midnight created a narrow line of convection, which tapped some strong winds aloft to cause localized damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines on Ellington Avenue were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the area of Route 169 and Woodstock Road, numerous trees were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blown down by thunderstorm winds fell on a house on Brayman Hollow Road.
